title: This is the most useful n8n workflow I have ever built...
summary: NeuralNine demonstrates two practical n8n workflows: one that automatically converts GitHub issues into pull requests, and another that turns Slack messages into GitHub issues. The video covers environment setup and implementation details for both automation pipelines.
writeup: A step-by-step build of a fully autonomous coding agent that chains N8N, Claude Code (headless), OpenAI, Python, and GitHub webhooks into two interlocked workflows: one that fires whenever a GitHub issue is created and implements the requested change via Claude Code before opening a pull request, and a second that converts a plain Slack message into a GitHub issue to kick off the first. The through-line is the practical staffing problem of letting non-technical collaborators describe work in natural language while a developer only reviews and merges the resulting pull request.
key_points: The system operates through two N8N workflows: a GitHub-issue trigger that runs a local Python script invoking Claude Code in headless mode to implement changes, commit, push, and open a pull request, then notifies Slack on success; and a Slack-trigger workflow that uses an OpenAI-powered agent to convert a freeform Slack message into a structured GitHub issue, which in turn fires the first workflow.

Claude Code runs in headless mode as the actual code-writing agent, meaning N8N does not do the coding but merely orchestrates the trigger and the local execution environment where Claude Code operates autonomously on the repository.

The N8N 'Execute Command' node, which invokes the local Python script and Claude Code, is classified as an unsafe node and requires the self-hosted variant of N8N with the environment variable N8N_ENABLE_UNSAFE_CORE_NODES set to true; the N8N cloud version cannot execute local commands and therefore cannot replicate this architecture.

GitHub webhook delivery requires a publicly reachable URL, so local development depends on a tunnel tool such as ngrok (run as 'ngrok http 5678') to expose the N8N port; production deployments would use a server with a real public IP or domain instead.

The live demo validates the full chain with two concrete tasks: adding the first 10 Fibonacci numbers to a README file (triggered by a GitHub issue) and generating a minimal Flask app with app.py and index.html (triggered by a Slack message that the OpenAI agent converts into an issue), both delivered as merged-ready pull requests with no manual code intervention.

The stated real-world motivation is enabling non-technical co-founders to request small UI changes by chatting with a bot or filing issues, with a developer's only obligation being to review and approve the resulting pull request, reducing interruption cost for routine incremental work.
